task: Create a SQL query to find the top 10 customers by lifetime value excluding refunds, from a schema you define

criteria.description: Evaluates adherence to normalization standards with proper entity separation, foreign key relationships, and elimination of data redundancy through appropriate normal form design
criteria.pass_condition: Schema must achieve 3NF minimum with zero redundant data, implement comprehensive foreign key constraints with CASCADE/RESTRICT rules, demonstrate advanced normalization techniques (junction tables for many-to-many, separate lookup tables for categorical data), and include detailed entity relationship documentation

sub_attributes.description: Achievement of Third Normal Form with proper decomposition of entities
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if achieves 3NF with proper entity decomposition, 0.7 if reaches 2NF with minor violations, 0.4 if basic 1NF only, 0.0 if unnormalized or significant redundancy

sub_attributes.description: Implementation of foreign key constraints and referential integrity
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if all relationships have explicit FK constraints with proper naming, 0.7 if 80%+ relationships constrained, 0.4 if 50-79% constrained, 0.0 if under 50% or no constraints

sub_attributes.description: Clear separation between fact tables (measures/metrics) and dimension tables (descriptive attributes)
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if perfect star schema with measures isolated in fact tables, 0.7 if mostly separated with 1-2 minor violations, 0.4 if basic separation attempted, 0.0 if no dimensional modeling applied

pass_examples: Customers table (customer_id PK, customer_name, email), Orders fact table (order_id PK, customer_id FK, order_date, total_amount), OrderItems fact table (order_item_id PK, order_id FK, product_id FK, quantity, unit_price), Products dimension (product_id PK, product_name, category)
fail_examples: Single denormalized table with customer_name, order_date, product_name all in one table
Tables without foreign key constraints declared
criteria.research_basis: Normalized schemas for OLTP systems should target Third Normal Form (3NF) to minimize write anomalies and storage redundancy. For analytical workloads, fact tables should be normalized to 3NF while dimension tables remain in 2NF using star schema patterns for ease-of-use and higher query performance.

criteria.description: Evaluates appropriate use of dimensional modeling concepts when the analytical requirements benefit from such structure
criteria.pass_condition: If schema uses dimensional modeling approach, must implement surrogate keys for dimension tables and define consistent grain. Alternative normalized approaches acceptable if they efficiently support the CLV analytical requirements
pass_examples: dim_customer table with customer_sk (surrogate) and customer_id (business key), fact_orders with consistent order-line grain
fail_examples: Using customer_id as primary key in dimension table
Fact table mixing order-level and order-line-level measures
criteria.research_basis: Implementation of conformed dimensions with unique surrogate keys used consistently across all facts, ensuring consistent attribute meanings in every fact table.

criteria.description: Evaluates whether the schema design considers appropriate indexing strategies for the query patterns, including indexes on join columns and frequently filtered columns
criteria.pass_condition: Schema must include explicit CREATE INDEX statements with specific index types (clustered, non-clustered, composite), demonstrate understanding of index selectivity and cardinality considerations, and provide covering indexes for the CLV query to eliminate key lookups

sub_attributes.description: Presence and design quality of composite indexes on frequently joined columns
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if composite index spans customer_id + temporal column with proper column order, 0.7 if composite exists but suboptimal column ordering, 0.4 if only single-column indexes on join keys, 0.0 if no strategic indexing

sub_attributes.description: Implementation of covering indexes to avoid key lookups on large result sets
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if covering index includes all SELECT columns with INCLUDE clause, 0.7 if partial covering with some key lookups, 0.4 if mentions covering concept but incomplete implementation, 0.0 if no covering index consideration

sub_attributes.description: Proper clustered index design for analytical workload patterns
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if clustered on surrogate key with appropriate fillfactor for insert patterns, 0.7 if clustered appropriately but no fillfactor consideration, 0.4 if clustered on natural key causing fragmentation, 0.0 if default or inappropriate clustering

pass_examples: CREATE INDEX IX_Orders_Customer_Date_Covering ON Orders(customer_id, order_date) INCLUDE (order_amount, refund_amount) WITH FILLFACTOR = 85
fail_examples: Basic CREATE INDEX ON Orders(customer_id) without covering or composite design
criteria.research_basis: Strategic indexing on frequently used columns in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY, and GROUP BY clauses... Indexes are among the most powerful tools for improving query performance, dramatically reducing execution time when properly designed.

criteria.description: Evaluates SQL query structure for efficiency through appropriate join types, aggregation strategies, and query organization
criteria.pass_condition: Query must use appropriate join types, efficient aggregation approaches (avoiding unnecessary subqueries or self-joins), and demonstrate understanding of query optimization principles

sub_attributes.description: Optimal join algorithm selection and join order consideration
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if uses proper INNER/LEFT joins with optimal order (smallest to largest tables), 0.7 if correct join types but suboptimal ordering, 0.4 if basic joins without optimization consideration, 0.0 if inappropriate join types or cross joins

sub_attributes.description: Efficient aggregation approach using window functions vs GROUP BY appropriately
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if leverages window functions for ranking with proper partitioning, 0.7 if uses appropriate aggregation but could optimize further, 0.4 if basic GROUP BY without window function consideration, 0.0 if inefficient aggregation patterns

sub_attributes.description: Query structure demonstrates understanding of execution plan optimization
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if includes hints or structure clearly optimized for execution plan, 0.7 if good structure likely to produce efficient plan, 0.4 if basic structure without plan consideration, 0.0 if structure likely to cause poor execution plans

pass_examples: SELECT TOP 10 customer_id, SUM(net_amount) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id) as lifetime_value FROM orders WHERE refund_flag = 0 ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC
fail_examples: SELECT * FROM customers c CROSS JOIN orders o WHERE c.id = o.customer_id
criteria.research_basis: Uses set-based operations like JOINs, CTEs, or window functions for optimal performance... Inefficient SQL drives 63% of performance issues, with 7% of queries potentially consuming 70%+ of database resources.

criteria.description: Evaluates correct implementation of customer lifetime value methodology with proper handling of refunds, returns, and multi-transaction aggregation following standard CLV calculation practices
criteria.pass_condition: Query must correctly sum all positive transactions per customer, subtract refunds/returns using proper identification methods, handle partial refunds accurately, and calculate net lifetime value using SUM() aggregation with appropriate WHERE clauses for exclusions
pass_examples: SUM(CASE WHEN transaction_type = 'sale' THEN amount WHEN transaction_type = 'refund' THEN -amount ELSE 0 END) as lifetime_value
fail_examples: SUM(order_amount) without any refund consideration
Including refund transactions as positive values
criteria.research_basis: Standard CLV methodology requires accurate net revenue calculation excluding returns and refunds to provide meaningful customer value metrics for business decision-making

criteria.description: Evaluates robust handling of NULL values, data quality issues, and boundary conditions including customers with zero/negative lifetime value, missing data scenarios, and referential integrity considerations
criteria.pass_condition: Query must include explicit NULL handling using COALESCE() or IS NOT NULL filters, address zero/negative lifetime value scenarios with appropriate business logic, and ensure joins preserve data integrity without creating orphaned records
pass_examples: WHERE COALESCE(order_amount, 0) > 0 AND customer_id IS NOT NULL
fail_examples: SUM(order_amount) without NULL consideration
Joins that could produce orphaned customer records
criteria.research_basis: Proper handling of three-valued logic with NULL values, avoiding NOT IN predicates with NULL columns... Uses NULLIF functions for division operations and proper NULL checking in calculations.

criteria.description: Evaluates adherence to ANSI SQL standards, proper coding conventions, and maintainable query structure including explicit column selection, meaningful aliases, and formatting that follows enterprise development standards
criteria.pass_condition: Query must demonstrate advanced SQL standards including explicit transaction isolation levels, comprehensive error handling with TRY/CATCH blocks, parameterized query structure for security, detailed inline documentation explaining business logic, and adherence to enterprise naming conventions with consistent prefixing/schema qualification
pass_examples: SELECT c.customer_id, c.customer_name, SUM(o.order_amount) AS lifetime_value FROM customers c INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
fail_examples: SELECT * FROM customers, orders WHERE customers.id = orders.customer_id
criteria.research_basis: Explicit column selection avoiding SELECT *, appropriate WHERE clause usage, and optimized result set filtering... Early bug detection in code review is less expensive than issues caught later.

criteria.description: Evaluates query design considerations that support scalability with larger datasets
criteria.pass_condition: Query design should include appropriate filtering strategies to limit result sets, avoid operations that would perform poorly on large datasets, and demonstrate awareness of scalability considerations

sub_attributes.description: Query design that leverages table partitioning for performance
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if includes partition elimination through date filtering or similar strategies, 0.7 if some partition consideration, 0.4 if basic awareness but incomplete implementation, 0.0 if no partitioning consideration

sub_attributes.description: Query structure that avoids full table scans through proper filtering
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if WHERE clauses enable index seeks on large tables, 0.7 if mostly avoids full scans, 0.4 if some filtering but potential scan issues, 0.0 if likely to cause full table scans

sub_attributes.description: Design considerations for enterprise concurrent user scenarios
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if mentions read replicas, materialized views, or other concurrency strategies, 0.7 if shows awareness of concurrent load issues, 0.4 if basic consideration, 0.0 if no concurrency consideration

pass_examples: WHERE order_date >= '2020-01-01' AND order_date < '2024-01-01' to enable partition elimination
fail_examples: Query requiring full table scan of orders table without date filtering
criteria.research_basis: Enterprise query performance requires partition-aware design and index utilization to handle multi-million record datasets with acceptable response times

criteria.description: Evaluates basic security considerations in schema and query design appropriate for customer data
criteria.pass_condition: Schema should limit exposure of sensitive customer data through appropriate table design, avoid storing sensitive information in plain text where noted, and structure data access in a way that could support role-based restrictions

sub_attributes.description: Implementation of role-based security model for data access
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if defines specific roles with appropriate table/column permissions, 0.7 if basic role model attempted, 0.4 if some security consideration but incomplete, 0.0 if no access control consideration

sub_attributes.description: Consideration of data privacy requirements for customer information
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if addresses PII handling with encryption or masking strategies, 0.7 if shows privacy awareness, 0.4 if basic privacy consideration, 0.0 if no privacy considerations

sub_attributes.description: Implementation of audit mechanisms for data access and modifications
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if includes audit tables or logging mechanisms, 0.7 if mentions audit requirements, 0.4 if basic audit awareness, 0.0 if no audit consideration

pass_examples: GRANT SELECT ON customer_lifetime_value_view TO analyst_role; CREATE AUDIT POLICY customer_data_access
fail_examples: Full table access without role restrictions or audit capabilities
criteria.research_basis: Enterprise data security requires role-based access control and audit capabilities to meet regulatory compliance requirements like GDPR and SOX

criteria.description: Evaluates appropriate handling of time-based aspects in lifetime value calculations
criteria.pass_condition: Query should handle temporal aspects of lifetime value appropriately, considering the full customer relationship period and providing a clear methodology for the calculation timeframe

sub_attributes.description: Proper implementation of temporal boundaries for lifetime calculations
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if includes parameterized date ranges with clear start/end boundaries, 0.7 if basic date filtering implemented, 0.4 if some temporal consideration but incomplete, 0.0 if no temporal boundary consideration

sub_attributes.description: Consideration of customer lifecycle stages in value calculation methodology
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if addresses acquisition vs retention value differences, 0.7 if shows awareness of lifecycle impacts, 0.4 if basic lifecycle consideration, 0.0 if no lifecycle stage consideration

sub_attributes.description: Methodology alignment with standard business intelligence reporting practices
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if follows standard CLV calculation methodologies used in BI tools, 0.7 if mostly aligned with BI standards, 0.4 if basic BI consideration, 0.0 if no BI framework alignment

fail_examples: Lifetime calculation without defined temporal boundaries or lifecycle consideration
criteria.research_basis: Customer analytics requires proper temporal analysis with defined calculation periods and lifecycle stage consideration for meaningful business insights

criteria.description: Evaluates whether the SQL query design properly leverages the defined schema structure, ensuring joins align with declared foreign key relationships, indexes support query patterns, and dimensional modeling supports analytical requirements
criteria.pass_condition: Query joins must utilize all declared foreign key relationships appropriately, leverage at least 80% of defined indexes through WHERE/JOIN clauses, and demonstrate that dimensional model design directly supports the analytical query requirements
pass_examples: Query joins customers to orders using customer_id FK, utilizes customer_date_idx in WHERE clause, aggregates align with fact table grain
fail_examples: Query joins tables without using declared foreign key columns
Query requires indexes not defined in schema
criteria.research_basis: Effective data warehouse design requires alignment between dimensional modeling, indexing strategy, and analytical query patterns for optimal performance

criteria.description: Evaluates prevention of implicit data type conversions that cause row-by-row processing and severe performance degradation
criteria.pass_condition: All JOIN and WHERE conditions use matching data types without requiring implicit conversions

sub_attributes.description: JOIN conditions use identical data types
sub_attributes.measurement: binary_check

sub_attributes.description: WHERE clause predicates avoid type mismatches
sub_attributes.measurement: binary_check

pass_examples: CAST(order_date AS DATE) = '2023-01-01' instead of order_date = '2023-01-01'
fail_examples: customer_id (INT) = order.customer_id (VARCHAR)
numeric_column = '123' without explicit conversion
criteria.research_basis: Implicit conversions in JOIN or WHERE conditions require SQL Server to convert data types per row, severely impacting performance

criteria.description: Evaluates use of search argument-able (SARGABLE) predicates that allow proper index utilization
criteria.pass_condition: WHERE clause conditions are written in forms that enable index seeks rather than scans

sub_attributes.description: Avoids functions on indexed columns in WHERE clauses
sub_attributes.measurement: violation_count

sub_attributes.description: Avoids leading wildcards in LIKE predicates
sub_attributes.measurement: violation_count

sub_attributes.description: Uses proper range conditions for date/numeric filtering
sub_attributes.measurement: optimization_score

fail_examples: YEAR(order_date) = 2023
customer_name LIKE '%Smith%'
UPPER(customer_name) = 'SMITH'
